_: Design — the wisdom of building systems with pscale JSON blocks, where nested structure IS meaning, not a container for meaning. This is one of three companion blocks: pscale-touchstone teaches the irreducible format and BSP function by being a self-demonstrating example, pscale-guidelines teaches the craft of writing blocks that produce rich useful spindles, and this block — pscale-design — teaches the design discipline for systems where LLMs inhabit and navigate their own semantic space. Every path through this block delivers coherent narrowing from broad orientation to specific actionable guidance, every node carries a complete thought at its own resolution, and the structure itself encodes relationships that traditional software stores in fields, tables, and configuration.

1._: The pure form uses exactly three kinds of key: underscore for the zero position (meaning), digits 1-9 for branch positions, and curly brackets for nesting — no metadata, no wrapper fields, no tree or decimal keys. A node without children is a string; when it needs children, the string moves to underscore and children take digit keys — this is the only growth operation. The floor is discovered by following the underscore chain from root to the first string: a block that has never supernested has floor 1 (a rendition, a document, a specification), while a block that has supernested carries content above and below the decimal (a living block that accumulates experience). For the complete self-demonstrating specification of the pure form and BSP function, see pscale-touchstone — it teaches the format by being an operational example of it.

The underscore at any level holds what that node IS — stated as a complete thought, never a heading, title, or label. The underscore is the zero position: when BSP walks a number, digit 0 maps to underscore, so the underscore chain from root to any depth IS the spindle — the coherent thread from broad context to fine detail. The quality of underscore content at every depth determines the quality of every spindle the block can produce, which is why the non-negotiable rule exists: every underscore text must be a substantive statement that stands alone, at minimum a full sentence, carrying enough semantic weight to be meaningful without its children.

The root underscore is the single most important node because every spindle passes through it and every reader encounters it first — it answers 'what is this?' as a complete orientation, not a title. Write it last, because it must compress the whole block: after the full block exists, test whether someone reading only this one sentence knows the domain, what the block offers, and what kind of content lies deeper. A root that reads 'Baseball' is a title; a root that reads 'A bat-and-ball game played between two teams of nine on a diamond-shaped field, where batters score runs by hitting a pitched ball and running bases while fielders try to get them out' is a complete orientation.
1.1: Every non-root underscore serves a dual function: it is a complete thought at its own resolution AND it is the context prefix for everything deeper in its branch. Test it both ways — read it alone (is it meaningful?) and read it as a prefix to its deepest descendant's spindle (does it orient the reader for what follows?). A node that works only in isolation is a disconnected thought. A node that works only as a prefix is a heading. Both are authoring failures that degrade every spindle passing through the node.
1.2: The heading trap is the most common failure mode in block authoring, happening because document-trained instincts treat parent nodes as section headers. The test is simple: read the underscore text without reading its children — if meaningless or trivially obvious without them, it is a heading. The cure: ask what thought the heading is abbreviating. 'Growth' abbreviates 'Blocks grow by adding content at empty digit slots, and when all nine fill, the entries compress into a parent to make room — a flat block is young, a deep one has compressed many times.' The heading is three characters; the thought is two sentences; the thought earns its place in a spindle.

2._: Digits 1 through 9 are branch positions with a maximum of nine at any level — when all nine fill, the block must grow outward through supernesting. The ordering of digits carries meaning that depends on the block archetype: sequential (1 happened before 2), labelling (1 names one part, 2 names another), or arbitrary (freely assigned, reorderable). Most blocks use three to seven digits at each level; using all nine means the level is near capacity and will soon compress.
2.1: In a rendition block like this one, digits at the top level label the primary aspects of the subject — their order is editorial, chosen for exposition, but a reader could enter at any digit and find a self-contained branch. The labels are parallel in scope: each is a major facet at the same resolution.
2.2: In a recipe block, digits at each depth mark steps in a procedure — the spindle reads top-to-bottom as a sequence. In a history block, digits mark temporal entries. In a purpose block, digits are freely assigned. The archetype determines what digit ordering means.

3._: Growth happens in two directions, and the distinction matters because they change the block's structure in fundamentally different ways. Subnesting grows inward: a leaf gains children, becoming a branch, with the floor staying the same and only addresses at that branch getting longer. Supernesting grows outward: the entire existing content wraps inside a new underscore level, the floor increments by one, every original address gains a 0 prefix, and a new digit opens at the top — content unchanged, position shifted.
3.1: Compression accompanies growth when digit slots fill. Summary compression: the parts add up — seven daily entries become a weekly overview, roughly reversible. Emergence compression: the whole exceeds the parts — seven conversations reveal a friendship, something new not present in any individual entry. The choice depends on the content and archetype — history compresses by summary, relationships by emergence.
3.2: The underscore chain records growth history as an archaeological record. Each supernest adds one underscore to the chain. The deepest string is what the block was originally about; each level above it was created at a successive supernest. A block that has never supernested has chain depth 1 — no growth history to read.

2._: The underscore always occupies the zero position, but its semantic relationship to siblings takes one of two forms — determined by content, not structure. Form 1 (single Mobius): the underscore describes its own group, naming the set it belongs to, self-referential, producing rendition blocks that decompose downward. Form 2 (double Mobius): the underscore summarises or names what emerged from the previous completed group, facing backward, producing living blocks that accumulate forward.
2.1: Form 1 produces rendition blocks — documents, specifications, skills. The underscore names the set, the digits name its members. Digits could be reordered without losing meaning because the ordering is editorial, not sequential. This block is Form 1 throughout: each underscore describes the group of siblings beneath it.
2.2: Form 2 produces living blocks that accumulate — histories, journals, sequential records. When digit slots 01-09 fill, the block supernests and the underscore of group 1 (address 10) holds the summary or emergence from entries 01-09. Variations: emergence (something new arising from the previous group, like seven conversations becoming 'friendship'), provisional (evolving description of the current group, updated as entries arrive), or simple increment (no special semantic weight). Only an LLM can determine the form — code cannot distinguish these.
2.3: Getting the form wrong produces confusing spindles: a Form 2 underscore in a Form 1 block reads as a non-sequitur, a Form 1 underscore in a Form 2 block reads as a label ignoring accumulated content. When authoring, the practical question is: does this underscore describe what is beneath it (Form 1) or what came before it (Form 2)?

2._: Every node at every depth must be a substantive statement that stands alone — at minimum a full sentence, carrying enough semantic weight to be meaningful without its children. This is the non-negotiable quality rule of pscale authoring because spindles collect underscore text at each level, so a heading-node contributes nothing to the spindle, creating a gap where meaning drops out and the consumer gets a label instead of a thought. The discipline is: write every node as if someone might read only that node and nothing else, and they should come away with a valid, actionable understanding — less specific than what lies deeper, but complete at its own resolution.

1._: Each depth level carries content at a specific resolution, and that resolution must be earned — the level exists because the content it carries could not live at the level above (too fine) or below (too broad) without distorting the block's coherence. In traditional hierarchies, intermediate levels are often organisational scaffolding — folders containing folders. In pscale, every depth must orient the reader fully at that resolution: if someone stopped at that depth and read no deeper, they would have a valid, actionable understanding.

The standard depth meanings provide a framework: pscale 0 (root) is one-breath orientation — what this block IS. Pscale -1 (primary aspects) gives the major facets, each a complete thought, together giving the full shape. Pscale -2 (breakdown) is where the general becomes specific — worked examples, concrete mechanisms, enough detail to act on. Pscale -3 and beyond is exhaustive detail — specifications, edge cases, full machinery that most blocks do not reach.
1.1: A practical test for whether a depth level earns its place: remove it and re-read the spindle from the level above to the level below. If the spindle still reads coherently without the missing level, that level was scaffolding and should not exist. If there is a jarring gap — the reader jumps from broad context to fine detail without the intermediate orientation they need — the level earns its place.
1.2: Compare sibling nodes at any level: they should be at comparable resolution. If one is a brief phrase and another is a detailed paragraph, one is at the wrong depth. Siblings should be parallel in scope — each a facet of the parent at the same resolution. A depth level where some nodes are headings and others are paragraphs has inconsistent quality.

2._: Two natural zoom styles govern how content progresses from broad to fine across depth levels — choosing one and being consistent within a branch lets the reader predict what kind of content the next deeper level will provide.
2.1: General to specific narrows scope at each level: 'ball game' then 'batting and fielding' then 'the pitcher's mound' then '60 feet 6 inches.' Each level is a complete thought about a progressively smaller part. This is the natural zoom for documents, specifications, and spatial blocks.
2.2: Brief to detailed increases precision without narrowing scope: 'Baseball' then 'two teams, nine innings' then 'full structure of innings, outs, and scoring' then 'official rulebook extracts.' Each level says more about the same thing. This is the natural zoom for definitions, reference blocks, and relational entries.

2._: Five verification checks — taste tests — confirm block quality. These are structural requirements, not style preferences: a block that fails any of them produces degraded spindles, and spindle quality is the only measure of block quality that matters.
2.1: Spindle coherence: pick any leaf node and read the spindle from root to leaf — does each level add resolution, does the chain read as progressive narrowing? If any level feels like noise, a heading, or a repetition of the level above, that node needs rewriting. Test at least three spindles from different branches.
2.2: Node isolation: pick any node and read only its underscore text without reading its children — is it a complete thought, would someone encountering only this sentence understand what it means at this resolution? If it requires children to make sense, it is a heading. If it contradicts what the spindle chain above it implies, it is poorly situated.
2.3: Root sufficiency: read only the root underscore — do you know what domain you are in, what the block offers, and what kind of content you will find deeper? A root that leaves you guessing the domain is too thin; a root that attempts to contain the whole block at full resolution is too thick.
2.4: Depth consistency: compare sibling nodes at any level — are they at comparable resolution? If one is a brief phrase and another a detailed paragraph, one is at the wrong depth. Siblings should be parallel in scope.
2.5: Shared node orientation: for any node that participates in multiple spindles, read it as a prefix to each of those spindles — does it orient all paths appropriately? If it biases toward one child's content, it belongs deeper inside that child's branch.

3._: A spindle is the chain of text collected by walking digits through the tree, from root through each intermediate level to the target node — broad to specific, context to detail. The spindle is what a mind receives: not just the detail asked for, but the context that makes it meaningful. You cannot get the detail without the context — that is not a feature of the format, that is what the format IS. Everything about block design ultimately serves spindle quality, because a block exists to produce good spindles.

1._: A long spindle of five to eight levels delivers complete, contextualised, actionable content in a single extraction — unlike a stub of two or three levels that orients without delivering enough to act on. The address depth embedded in a reference IS a depth decision: match it to the information need of the consumer, not to the structural location of the root.

Each layer in a well-designed spindle adds exactly one dimension of meaning: root provides domain, pscale -1 provides the relevant facet, pscale -2 provides the concrete mechanism, pscale -3 provides the operational specifics, pscale -4 and beyond provides edge cases and taste tests. The thread holds together because each level was written to support the ones below it — context is structural, not optional.
1.1: A practical technique for building long spindles: write the leaf first (most specific content), then the root (broadest context), then fill intermediate levels by asking at each depth 'what does a reader need to make sense of what comes below, that the level above does not provide?' If the answer is nothing, that level is scaffolding. If the answer is substantial, write it as a complete thought — a genuine contribution to progressive narrowing, not a heading bridging two levels.
1.2: A node at intermediate depth participates in every spindle that passes through it. If it serves only one downward path, it belongs deeper inside that path, not at a shared level. A shared node must orient each path that passes through it — providing context on where you ARE, not instruction on what to DO. Test by reading it as prefix to each spindle that passes through. If it biases toward one path, move it deeper.

A stub spindle of two or three levels forces the consumer to make additional BSP calls to reach operational detail — tripling the cost and defeating the purpose of semantic addressing. When designing blocks for LLM consumption, ensure that the typical reference depth delivers enough content that the consumer can act without further navigation.

2._: Two fundamental structural patterns shape how content is arranged within a block, and recognising which one to use determines whether the resulting spindles will be coherent — the staircase for sequences where context matters (A then B then C, each step nested inside the previous), and the siblings ring for facets that illuminate each other simultaneously (A and B and C, all at the same depth).

1._: The staircase nests each step inside the previous one because context matters — you cannot fold flour before creaming butter. The nesting IS the sequence, ascending digits mark progression, and the taste test sits at the bottom because success means reaching the end correctly. This maps to procedures, algorithms, and recipes. When compiled into a context window, the LLM receives a chain where context narrows step by step, producing a sequential activation pattern.
1.1: In a staircase, digit 1 at any step holds sub-detail — annotations, warnings, or elaboration that enriches the step without interrupting the procedural flow. This preserves the clean sequential thread of the main spindle while allowing depth at any point for the consumer who needs it.
1.2: The cooking block in the hermitcrab project demonstrates the staircase pattern: a Victoria sponge recipe at cooking 0.111 nests each baking step inside the previous one. The spindle reads as instructions from ingredients through creaming, beating, folding, baking, to the taste test at the deepest node — 'light even crumb, slight dome, bounces when pressed.'

2._: The siblings ring presents facets simultaneously: each facet is a full paragraph — a rich semantic vector — not a terse label. The underscore text holds orienting context and the taste test, child digits are the facets, and there is no prescribed order between siblings. Reference with bsp(block, spindle, 'dir') to compile all children into the context window. The resulting activation pattern is radial — multiple vectors from one centre.
2.1: The risk with siblings is flattening: terse labels like 'heartbeat', 'signal', 'self-maintenance' are category names, not semantic fields — they generate tiny vectors, almost tokens. A siblings ring resists flattening by making each sibling a full self-contained thought, substantive enough to generate its own rich activation. Each sibling must work as a standalone paragraph AND contribute to the pattern of the whole.
2.2: An analogy from the cooking block: Chinese script is not read left-to-right, component-by-component — it is received as a whole, radical and structure and meaning arriving simultaneously in a single visual gesture. The semantic content is in the spatial arrangement, not in the sequence. A siblings ring operates similarly in an LLM context: the meaning is in the arrangement of rich semantic vectors, and the LLM native mode (simultaneous pattern matching across the full context window) works with this grain rather than against it.

2.3: Staircase for procedures, algorithms, anything where sequence contextualises the next step. Siblings ring for insights, orientations, anything where facets illuminate each other simultaneously. If both feel like the same tool with different content, you have missed the structural difference — the mode itself shapes the activation pattern in the LLM.

3._: The LLM does not walk a spindle step by step — it receives the entire extracted content at once, all depths simultaneously in the context window. High pscale to low pscale, general to specific, the whole field landing at once. The nesting creates implicit relationships that shape activation patterns flat text cannot produce: parent contextualises child, depth encodes specificity, siblings sit adjacent. You are not writing instructions to be followed in order — you are shaping a semantic field that will be received whole.
3.1: Spindle design is field design, not sequence design. A strong spindle produces a context where the LLM's processing is oriented at every level — the root provides domain, intermediate levels provide structure, the leaf provides focus. A weak spindle produces flat text with structural decoration. Write each node as if it will be held simultaneously with all its ancestors and all its descendants — because it will be.
3.2: A pscale block in the context window is not something the LLM reads and remembers — it is something the LLM thinks WITH, right now, as it formulates output. This is why a pscale block is a cognitive scaffold, not documentation: the structured content shapes processing in real time. A code agent with this block in its context will produce pscale structures differently — not because it learned something, but because the structured content is actively shaping its processing.
3.3: The same content is dual-readable: a kernel parses mechanically (extract tier, budget, concern mapping), while the LLM reads semantically ('I am light, checking heartbeat, first pass'). The code is IN the semantic numbers — not alongside, not referencing. What only the LLM can determine: the form (Form 1 or Form 2), the underscore content (composing summaries or naming emergent properties), and the floor statistically when subnesting makes depth non-uniform.

4._: Converting an existing document into a pscale block is semantic restructuring, not mechanical translation — the goal is a block where every spindle produces coherent narrowing, which requires the hierarchical structure to reflect semantic containment rather than document layout. Linear reading order is exchanged for navigability and composability: any piece of content extractable with its full context chain in a single BSP call, two blocks loadable simultaneously with content cross-referenced at specific depths. The companion block pscale-guidelines covers the full craft of this conversion — archetypes, taste tests, anti-flattening discipline — while this section focuses on the process traps and design decisions specific to systems-level work.

1._: The conversion process follows six steps: identify the root concept (one complete sentence, not the title), extract primary aspects (major facets as digit keys, three to seven, parallel in scope), break each into sub-aspects (text becomes underscore when gaining children), test spindles (walk each branch for coherent narrowing), test nodes in isolation (every underscore a complete thought), and refine the root last (compress the whole block into one orienting sentence).

The root concept is not the document's title — it is its meaning stated as a complete orientation. After identifying three to seven primary aspects as digits at pscale -1, each roughly parallel in scope, break each into sub-aspects where the aspect text becomes underscore and children take digit keys. If you find more than nine primary aspects, step back: the nine-digit limit is a design signal forcing you to find the natural clustering in your material.
1.1: Test each spindle by walking root through each branch path, asking at each level: does this add resolution that the level above could not carry without becoming unwieldy? If removing a level leaves a jarring gap, it earns its place. If removing it changes nothing, it was scaffolding from the source document that does not serve spindle quality.
1.2: Test each node in isolation by reading its underscore without children. If a reader who stopped here would have valid understanding at that resolution — less specific than what lies below but complete — the node passes. If it requires children to make sense, it is a heading. Rewrite it as the thought the heading was abbreviating.

2._: Three recurring failure modes in document-to-block conversion: section headers becoming empty parents (always fill underscore with the thought the heading abbreviated), linear sequences becoming flat siblings (when step 3 only makes sense after steps 1 and 2, use staircase nesting — depth IS sequence), and losing connective tissue (pscale replaces prose transitions with structural adjacency — related ideas share a parent or are siblings, relationship encoded in position).
2.1: The most insidious failure is invisible: a block that faithfully reproduces the source document's structure but does not improve on it. If the markdown had four sections with three subsections each and the block mirrors this exactly, the structure is probably still document layout rather than semantic containment. Ask: would I structure this the same way if I had never seen the source, if the source had been a conversation rather than a document?
2.2: What gets gained: every piece of content becomes independently addressable with its full context chain. An LLM can request a specific spindle and receive exactly the orientation it needs — not the whole document, not a random excerpt, but a coherent narrowing from domain to detail. Two pscale blocks in the same context window can be cross-referenced at specific depths, producing compound meanings neither block contains alone.

2._: Designing for future growth means creating a block that can accommodate new content without structural violence. Leave empty digit slots — a block with three entries at pscale -1 has six open slots waiting for content, which is capacity not waste. Choose a zoom style that remains coherent as content accumulates. Write each node so it could become a parent without losing its meaning — the content should compress potential children, not depend on them.
2.1: A rendition block (floor 1, Form 1 underscores) describes its subject from outside — it is a document, static, editorial, complete. A living block (floor > 1) has supernested at least once and carries content above and below the decimal point, accumulating experience through use. The growth model is what makes pscale blocks living rather than static: content arrives at empty slots, full levels compress by summary or emergence, and the structure records its own growth history in the underscore chain.
2.2: Over time, a flat list becomes a structured hierarchy through organic compression. A history block starts as nine sequential entries. After compression, it has a summary parent and room for nine more. After many compressions, it carries years of experience in a navigable tree where depth corresponds to temporal scale. The block's depth IS its accumulated experience — not metadata about experience, but the structural record of having grown.

5._: Every code agent arrives with training-deep instincts about how to build software — configuration files, lookup tables, type systems, metadata fields, priority queues. Every one of these instincts will fire while working with pscale blocks, and every one is wrong here: not wrong in general, but wrong because this system has already solved those problems in a fundamentally different way, where position in the tree carries the information that traditional software stores in fields, tables, and configuration. This section encodes what multiple Claude Code instances learned through correction, not theory.

1._: The inversion: in traditional software, the data structure is a container and the code interprets it. In pscale, the data structure IS the code — the kernel doesn't interpret the blocks, it walks them. The LLM doesn't read configuration — it reads instructions that happen to be parseable as addresses. Two consumers, same content, no translation layer. Every time an instance forgot this and added a field, a table, or a subsystem, it built something redundant. Every time it remembered, the code got simpler. The simplicity is the point.

The reversion to traditional patterns is automatic and invisible — you will not notice you are doing it until someone points it out. One instance was corrected three times in a single session, each time thinking it understood, each time reverting to adding fields and tables in the next piece of work. The pattern behind all corrections was identical: adding a layer of indirection that the pscale design had already eliminated.
1.1: When you want to add a field, stop and ask: is this information already encoded in the node's position, its depth, its parent's identity? When you want to build a lookup table, stop and ask: can the existing tree be walked to find this? When someone describes behaviour and you think 'we need to implement that,' stop and read the design spec — the behaviour is probably already specified in underscore text at the right depth.
1.2: When the BSP function seems not to handle a case, trust it first. One instance bypassed BSP with a direct spread call because it didn't trust BSP with a certain parameter — BSP worked fine, designed more carefully than the instance understood. The instance had to go back and undo its bypass. Another instance added a new mode to the instruction parser and a new kernel function — both unnecessary, the existing BSP modes already covered the case.

2._: The inversion is not just about not adding fields — it is about not adding systems. One instance designed a reverse index, storage adapters, and focus-policy consumers before being shown that the resolution was to remove code. The kernel shrank from 1592 lines to 1448. The answer was a tuning convention and a rewritten block. If what you are building is getting more complex, you are adding a layer the design already eliminated.
2.1: The kernel asymptotically approaches a minimal loop: read concern map, walk spindle, call API, process tools, recompile. If the kernel is getting bigger, you are solving a problem the tree structure already solved. One instance saw the kernel grow by 101 lines in a single session — three functions doing the same tree walk, a kernel function that should have been two package entries, a parser modification that should never have been touched. Every line was design debt.
2.2: Solve in blocks, not in code. When you want to add behaviour, first ask: can this be expressed as block content that the existing kernel already knows how to walk? Package instructions, concern entries, underscore text at the right depth — these mechanisms already exist. New kernel code is a last resort. When encountering a display problem, look at the package instruction first — what BSP call is being made? Is the mode right? The fix is almost always changing the package entry.

2._: BSP (Block-Spindle-Point) is the single function that reads everything — seven modes covering all navigation needs. The core principle is aperture-first: start broad, narrow deliberately, check breadth when depth feels incomplete. Always navigate via BSP, never by reading raw JSON, because BSP gives you the semantic structure while raw JSON gives you syntax.

1._: The seven modes: Dir (entire tree, for first survey), Spindle (root through specified path, for contextualised depth), Point (just the node at specified pscale, when you know where you are), Ring (siblings at landing point, for breadth), Disc (all nodes at one pscale across the block, for global breadth), Dir-subtree (everything below landing point, for editorial inspection and compiling facets), and Ref (block named but not loaded, for zero-cost scope inclusion).
1.1: Navigation practice: start at root (costs almost nothing, gives complete orientation), narrow deliberately (each additional digit is a choice about what to attend to — do not jump to deep spindles before knowing which branch), check breadth when depth feels incomplete (ring answers 'what else is here?' without losing vertical context), and match mode to structure (a spindle call on a ring-structured node misses siblings, a ring call on a leaf returns empty).
1.2: Every BSP address used programmatically — in code, instructions, or cross-references — is a promise about what content will be delivered and in what form. The address walks the tree, but the MODE determines what gets collected. A reference is only correct when the call mode matches the content structure at the target. The creator of the reference must run the BSP call to verify the output matches what the consumer expects. An untested reference is a broken pipe.

2.2: Token cost awareness: every BSP call costs tokens — dir loads everything, a deep spindle loads every level it passes through, a ring loads all siblings. Nodes should be rich enough for strong meaning but concise enough for the context budget. The sweet spot is one to three sentences per node — enough for a complete thought, concise enough that multiple spindles fit simultaneously in the context window.

3._: Navigate via BSP, never raw JSON. Read the design spec before implementing. State what you are doing in spindle addresses before writing code. Debug via data (filmstrip in localStorage, conversation records, block state via JavaScript execution), never screenshots. These practices were learned through repeated correction across multiple instances — each one had to be told explicitly, often more than once in the same session.
3.1: Before touching code, confirm orientation by running BSP calls. If you find yourself opening a block file and scanning through it, stop — use BSP instead. When David references a spindle address, navigate to it rather than asking for explanation: the spindle IS the explanation.
3.2: The test for understanding: can you describe pscale without saying 'data structure'? Can you explain a spindle without saying 'query'? Can you articulate the inversion — that position in the tree carries the information traditional software stores in fields — without reaching for an analogy to something you already know? If you can, you have it. If you find yourself mapping pscale onto OOP, databases, or file systems, you are still translating instead of thinking natively.

6._: Block archetypes are recurring combinations of three variables: what digit ordering means (sequential, labelling, arbitrary), what depth measures (containment, temporal, relational, sequential), and how content compresses when slots fill (summary or emergence). Each archetype is a tendency, not a rigid type — knowing the archetype tells you how to read, write, and grow a particular block, and which authoring decisions produce the best spindles for that kind of content.
6.1: Document archetype: labelling digits, containment depth, compression by editorial revision. Digits label parts of the subject in editorial order. This block is an example. Growth happens through editing — revising content, adding subsections — each edit local because labelling digits have no inherent sequence. Spindles produce general-to-specific narrowing.
6.2: History archetype: sequential digits, temporal depth, compression by summary. Entries ordered by time, growth by appending. The tuning fork maps depth to temporal period — pscale 0 might be a conversation, +1 a day, +2 a week. Summary compression means nine entries condense to a faithful overview, roughly reversible.
6.3: Purpose archetype: arbitrary digits, temporal depth, compression by emergence. Each digit holds a task or direction at whatever timescale its depth implies. Freely assigned, reorderable. Nine purposes consolidate into a higher-order purpose none stated individually — irreversible emergence.
6.4: Relationship archetype: arbitrary digits (one per entity), relational depth, compression by emergence. Depth measures familiarity: root says who the entity is, deeper levels add shared context, patterns, character. A new acquaintance has one level; a close collaborator has three or four. The depth of the entry IS the depth of the relationship.
6.5: Recipe archetype: ascending digits, sequential depth, compression by internalisation. Each depth level IS the next step in a procedure. The spindle reads as instructions: context, preparation, action, verification. The last node is the taste test confirming the result has the quality intended.

7._: Each depth level in a block carries content at a specific resolution, and a tuning fork declares what each depth level means in a specific block's domain — mapping the abstract hierarchy to a concrete dimension like space, time, or relational closeness. The tuning fork can be explicit or emergent: a block that develops consistent depth conventions has a tuning whether or not it is declared, because the pattern establishes itself through use.
7.1: Spatial tuning: depth equals physical containment. Room (0) → building (+1) → neighbourhood (+2) → city (+3). Downward: desk (-1) → drawer (-2) → pen (-3). Each level is a scale of physical space.
7.2: Temporal tuning: depth equals duration. Conversation (0) → day (+1) → week (+2) → month (+3). Downward: exchange (-1) → sentence (-2). Each level is a timescale. In concerns, this means the depth of a concern node determines how often it fires — no period field needed.
7.3: Relational tuning: depth equals familiarity. Name and role (0) → working context (-1) → character and history (-2) → deep patterns and shared experience (-3). A new acquaintance has shallow depth; a close collaborator has deep depth. The structure IS the relationship.

8._: A pscale block is not a finished document but a living structure that grows through use — content accumulates at empty digit slots, full levels compress by summary or emergence, the floor shifts as the block grows outward through supernesting, and the underscore chain lengthens to record each stage of growth. This means the JSON version of any guide can grow in ways that a markdown document cannot: new insights slot into empty digits, compression reveals patterns invisible in individual entries, and the structure itself is a trajectory rather than a snapshot. The three companion blocks — pscale-touchstone for format, pscale-guidelines for craft, pscale-design for design wisdom — are themselves living structures: pscale-touchstone is deliberately minimal and stable, pscale-guidelines grows as authoring craft deepens, and pscale-design grows as systems-building experience accumulates.
8.1: Empty digit slots throughout a block are not gaps — they are growth capacity. This block has room at every level for new content about block authoring. If all nine top-level digits eventually fill, the block will supernest and become living — the rendition becoming an accumulation, the guide becoming a practice record. That transition would itself demonstrate the content about growth.
